data.title: Trending stocks this week: Wall Street extends gains on softer inflation, trade optimism
data.description:
Wall Street capped its strongest week since early August, buoyed by robust corporate earnings, renewed optimism over U.S.–China trade relations, and a brief reprieve for investors contending with the ongoing government shutdown that has slowed the flow of economic data.

For the week, the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) gained +1.9%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) added +2.2%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced +2.3%.

The week’s standout moment came on Friday with the release of the long-awaited September consumer price index (CPI) report, which showed both headline and core inflation rising less than expected month-over-month. Notably, core CPI posted its slowest pace of growth since June, easing investor concerns about persistent inflation.

The softer CPI print bolstered expectations that the Federal Reserve will move ahead with another 25-basis-point rate cut at next week’s policy meeting.

Adding to the week’s market momentum, officials confirmed that President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping will meet during Trump’s upcoming Asia visit — a development seen as a potential step toward easing trade tensions between the world’s two largest economies.

Here’s what caught investor attention this week:

Netflix (NFLX) slumped after its quarterly profit took a hit from an expense stemming from a dispute with Brazilian tax authorities.

Amazon’s (AMZN) Web Services suffered a major outage on Monday, disrupting numerous websites, apps, and online games. The company later said the hours-long disruption was caused by a bug in its automation software, which triggered widespread system failures affecting services ranging from Signal to smart home devices.

Intel (INTC) shares rose after the chipmaker reported a return to profitability in its third-quarter results and issued a robust revenue forecast, signaling a potential turnaround. Its Client Computing Group, which produces chips for laptops and desktops, saw revenues climb 5% year-over-year to $8.5 billion, contributing to Intel’s solid sales beat.

Newmont (NEM) fell even after reporting better than expected Q3 adjusted earnings and revenues, as lower production levels prevented the precious metals miner from taking full advantage of record-high gold prices. Q3 average realized price of gold increased to $3,539/oz from $2,518/oz a year earlier, but attributable gold production fell 15% to 1.42 million oz from 1.67 million oz in the prior-year period.

Ford (F) rose after reporting Q3 results that surpassed profit expectations. Traders also considered the company’s plan to increase pickup truck production by 50,000 units, despite an expected $1.5 billion–$2 billion EBIT hit from the Novelis aluminum plant fire.

Tesla (TSLA) fell after profit plunged despite a record quarter of vehicle sales. The electric vehicle giant reported EPS of $0.50 vs. $0.62 a year ago on revenue that grew 11.6% year-over-year to $28.1 billion. Operating income decreased 40% Y/Y to $1.6B, resulting in a 5.8% operating margin. The company delivered 497,099 vehicles in Q3, up 7% Y/Y, as buyers rushed to capitalize on the end of the $7,500 government tax credit.

Quantum computing stocks jumped following a Wall Street Journal report that the Trump administration was negotiating equity stakes in several companies. However, a later report from CNBC denied that talks were in progress. Still, IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti Computing (RGTI) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) all skyrocketed on Thursday.

International Business Machines (IBM) fell after it reported disappointing revenue in two key software categories, including its closely watched Red Hat unit.

General Motors (GM) had its best day in nearly six years on Tuesday after the automaker delivered a top- and bottom-line beat in the third quarter. A combination of upbeat profit guidance and tariff outlook for the coming year gave stock an additional boost. Tariffs are expected to cost the company $4.5B which is $500M less than previously anticipated.

Valero Energy (VLO) gained attention after announcing plans to proceed with the closure of its 145,000-barrel-per-day Benicia refinery in California by next spring, following discussions with state officials about potential options to keep the facility operational.

Meme stocks staged a resurgence over the past week, with Beyond Meat (BYND) soaring more than 1,000% from a recent low at one point. Other popular names, including Krispy Kreme (DNUT), GoPro (GPRO), and iRobot (IRBT), also logged intraday gains of over 10%.

data.title: CenterPoint Energy restores power to about 124K customers in Houston after severe storms
data.description:
CenterPoint Energy on Saturday said it had restored power to approximately 124,000 customers in the greater Houston area following overnight storms that brought strong winds, heavy rain, and lightning.

According to a company update posted on X (formerly Twitter), fewer than 45,000 customers still remained without power. 

"Crews have been working diligently to restore power after last last night's severe weather packing strong winds, heavy rain and lightning. We'll continue working safely to restore remaining customers as quickly as possible," CenterPoint said. 

The overnight storms had earlier impacted nearly 169,000 customers at the height of their intensity, the company added. It has a total of 2.8M customers.

data.title: UK warehouse raid leads to record haul of illegal weight loss drugs
data.description:
The United Kingdom's healthcare regulator on Friday said it had made the world's largest seizure of illegal weight loss medicines after officers from the agency raided a warehouse in Northampton, finding thousands of unlicensed injection pens containing the ingredient in Eli Lilly's (NYSE:LLY) Zepbound and Mounjaro.

"This is the first illicit production facility for weight loss medicine discovered in the UK and is believed to be the largest single seizure of trafficked weight loss medicines ever recorded by a law enforcement agency worldwide," the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency ((MHRA)) said in a statement. 

The agency's raid uncovered more than 2,000 pens with tirzepatide - the ingredient in Lilly's (LLY) Zepbound and Mounjaro - and retatrutide, an experimental obesity drug also made by the company. 

"The street value of the finished weight loss products alone is estimated to be more than a quarter of a million pounds," the MHRA said.

"The site, on an industrial estate on the outskirts of Northampton, is believed to have been used for the large-scale manufacture, packaging, and distribution of unlicensed—and potentially deadly—weight loss products to customers," it added.

Eli Lilly (LLY) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

The blockbuster success of such weight loss and diabetes drugs has also led to a huge rise in counterfeit versions. Healthcare regulators across the globe, including the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicines Agency, have raised concerns over these illegal medicines.

data.title: Real estate stocks advance as October rate cut odds rise, earnings meet Wall Street expectations
data.description:
Real estate stocks moved higher as the sector posted financial results, which largely met Wall Street expectations, and the retail inflation report fueled October rate cut hopes.

The cooler-than-expected September retail inflation report seems to have confirmed that the Federal Reserve will continue its easing cycle in the next meeting.

"Inflation coming in weaker than expected further solidifies a continuation of the Fed's rate-cutting cycle, at least for the next two meetings," Skyler Weinand, chief investment officer, Regan Capital, said.

Currently, the odds of the Fed slashing interest rates by 25 basis points at the October 29 meeting stand at 98.3%, according to CME's FedWatch tool.

Meanwhile, the real estate sector saw its quarterly earnings largely meet Wall Street expectations. Here is a look at the week's major earnings releases:

  • Phillips Edison (PECO) - FFO in-line, Revenue beats
  • Elme Communities (ELME) - FFO misses, Revenue in-line
  • Healthpeak Properties (DOC) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Eastgroup Properties (EGP) - FFO in-line, Revenue in-line
  • Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) - FFO beats, Revenue misses
  • CBRE (CBRE) - Non-GAAP EPS of $1.61, Revenue beats
  • Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) - FFO in-line, Revenue beats
  • Veris Residential (VRE) - FFO beats, Revenue misses
  • Equity Lifestyle Properties (ELS) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Crown Castle (CCI) - AFFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Getty Realty (GTY) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Essential Properties (EPRT) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Agree Realty (ADC) - FFO beats, Revenue beats

Next week, the Fed will make its monetary policy announcement. The announcement can impact real estate stocks significantly.

Also, the focus will again be on the earnings releases. Next week is set to be a busy earnings week for the sector.

On Thursday, President Trump and President Xi Jinping are expected to meet face-to-face to address trade disputes. The event may prove a notable driver for stock price movements.

Additionally, the S&P/Case-Shiller House Price Index will be released on Tuesday. 

Company News

  • Entities tied to Makarora Management, along with Ares Alternative Credit funds, have agreed to acquire all outstanding shares of Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM) in an all-cash deal valued at about $2.1B.
  • Welltower (WELL) could announce a ~£4B deal to buy Barchester Healthcare, according to a posting on X from a Sky News reporter.
  • RE/MAX Holdings (RMAX) announced plans to expand in Morocco.
  • Barclays upgraded Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) to Overweight from Equalweight as the REIT's stock has lagged its net lease peers and earnings are well positioned to beat consensus estimates.
  • Lineage (LINE) has appointed Robb LeMasters CFO, effective November 10.
  • Getty Realty (GTY) raised its quarterly dividend by 3.2% to $0.485 per share, while Community Healthcare (CHCT) increased it by 0.5% to $0.475 per share.
  • Other major dividend announcements came from Apple Hospitality (APLE), SITE Centers (SITC), Postal Realty Trust (PSTL), Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI), Easterly Government (DEA), Highwoods Properties (HIW), and JBG SMITH Properties (JBGS).

Winners & Losers

Among the S&P 500 real estate stocks, CoStar Group (CSGP) was the biggest gainer of the week. The stock added 5.27% as the real estate company announced its continued support for Matterport Spaces across all media channels.

CoStar is scheduled to announce its Q3 earnings next week, and analysts expect a 19.15% year-over-year growth in revenue.

BXP (BXP), also scheduled to announce earnings next week, was the second-largest weekly gainer. The stock advanced 4.39% from the prior week.

Meanwhile, Public Storage (PSA), also set to report earnings next week, was the top decliner. The stock fell 1.75%.

For the broader real estate sector, Generation Income Properties (GIPR) (+29.69% W/W), Peakstone Realty Trust (PKST) (+13.71% W/W), and Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) (+11.31% W/W) led the weekly gainers.

Opendoor's gains come amid a return of the meme stocks rally.

Here is a look at the subsector performances:

data.title: Most S&P 500 industrial stocks deliver earnings beats this week
data.description:
Most S&P 500 industrial companies reporting this week surpassed earnings expectations, with only United Rentals (URI) and GE Vernova (GEV) missing estimates.

On the revenue front, six firms of the 21 industrial companies that reported results this week fell short of Wall Street forecasts. Quarter to date, just three of the 26 industrial companies that have released results have missed earnings targets, while seven have come up short on sales.

The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLI) has gained nearly 18% so far this year, outpacing the S&P 500’s roughly 16% advance.

Below are the latest quarterly reports from top industrial names this week:

GE Aerospace (GE) lifted its full-year guidance for the second consecutive quarter, reflecting sustained momentum from a strong air travel market. The company now projects adjusted earnings between $6.00 and $6.20 per share, up from its earlier range of $5.60 to $5.80, surpassing Wall Street’s consensus estimate of $5.92. GE Aerospace also raised its outlook for revenue growth to the high teens and boosted its forecasts for both operating profit and free cash flow.

3M (MMM) reported third-quarter earnings and revenue that exceeded expectations and raised its full-year profit outlook. The industrial conglomerate posted adjusted earnings of $2.19 per share, up 10% from a year ago and above Wall Street’s estimate of $2.07. Adjusted sales increased 4.1% to $6.3 billion, surpassing the expected $6.25 billion. 3M also raised its 2025 adjusted earnings guidance to $7.95–$8.05 per share from $7.75–$8.00, driven by stronger organic growth and improved margins.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) lowered its full-year free cash flow guidance, overshadowing stronger-than-expected quarterly earnings. The company now expects about $6.6 billion in free cash flow for 2025, tightening its prior forecast of $6.6 billion to $6.8 billion.

RTX (RTX) raised its full-year profit outlook after posting quarterly results that beat analyst estimates, driven by strong performance across its commercial aerospace and defense segments. The company now expects 2025 adjusted earnings of $6.10–$6.20 per share, up from the previous range topped at $5.95. Adjusted sales are projected between $86.5 billion and $87.0 billion, compared with the prior guidance of $84.75 billion–$85.5 billion.

Honeywell International (HON) beat Wall Street expectations with its third-quarter earnings and raised its full-year guidance ahead of the planned Solstice Advanced Materials spin-off. The industrial conglomerate posted net income of $1.9 billion, or $2.86 per share, up from $1.4 billion, or $2.17 per share a year ago, surpassing the GAAP estimate of $2.37 per share. Adjusted earnings of $2.82 per share also topped analysts’ forecast of $2.57.

For the upcoming week, Boeing (BA), Caterpillar (CAT) and United Parcel Service (UPS) are the notable industrial tickers scheduled to report results. For Boeing (BA), analysts expect a loss of -$2.38 per share on revenue of $22.13B. Caterpillar (CAT) is projected to earn $4.52 per share (-12.5% Y/Y) on $16.77B revenue.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:VIS), (FXR), (BATS:IYJ), (NYSEARCA:FIDU), and (NYSEARCA:RSPN).

data.title: Insider trades: American Express, Oracle among notable names this week
data.description:
This week’s insider transactions included sales from firms like American Express (NYSE:AXP), BlackRock (NYSE:BLK) and Snap (NYSE:SNAP). The following transactions occurred between October 20 and October 25.

Denise Pickett, president of enterprise shared services at American Express (AXP),  sold 5,000 shares for $350.73, amounting to $1.75 million. Pickett now holds 9,522 shares in the company. Separately, the company’s Chief Risk Officer, Douglas Tabish, let go of 2,515 shares for a total of $894,359. He now owns about 2,618.15 shares in the firm.

Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) CEO Clayton Magouyrk  sold 40,000 shares of the company at $276.64 for a total of $11.07 million, reducing his ownership to 154,030 shares post-sale. Meanwhile, the company’s EVP and Chief Accounting Officer, Maria Smith,  sold a total of 10,000 shares across two transactions at around $280 per share, for a combined value of $2.8 million. Smith retains 47,083 shares in the company.

Rachel Lord, senior managing director of BlackRock (BLK), sold shares worth $13.98M. She unloaded 12,000 shares at $1,165.04, reducing her holdings to 7,205 shares.

Snap (SNAP) Chief Business Officer Ajit Mohan  disposed of 28,238 shares at $7.83, worth $221K. Following the sale, Mohan still owns 5.44M shares of the company.

Richard Gerspacher, EVP of Capital Projects at Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC),  offloaded 63,198 shares for $429,282. The sale reduced Gerspacher’s holding to 77,691 shares.

MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) Director Jarrod Patten  purchased a total of 23,780 shares across two transactions, at an average price of about $97 per share, for a combined value of about $2.32 million. Following the purchases, his total ownership rose to 72,335 shares.

At CSX (NASDAQ:CSX), President and CEO Stephen Angel  acquired 55,000 shares at $36.87 apiece, for a total value of about $2.03 million, increasing his holdings to 58,234 shares.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: HP, Dell and Enphase Energy among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday, ending at a record high after cooler-than-expected consumer inflation report. For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) fell 0.2%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

J.P. Morgan cuts HP rating to Neutral

J.P. Morgan downgraded HP (NYSE:HPQ) to Neutral from Overweight and set a $30 price target on the stock.

"We are downgrading shares of HP Inc. from Overweight to Neutral as we see the company cycling past the favorable part of the volume and commodity cost cycle into a relatively tougher backdrop," said analysts led by Samik Chatterjee.

The analysts noted that the tough backdrop includes — 2026 representing the tail end of the Windows 10 replacement cycle, leading to tough comparisons for Commercial PC growth, continuation of a challenging macro environment, rising memory costs, among others.

Piper Sandler sees potential for upside from AI, PC refresh cycle for Dell

Piper Sandler initiated coverage on Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) with an Overweight rating, citing the potential for upside from artificial intelligence and a PC refresh cycle.

The brokerage also set a $172 price target on the stock.

“Positively, Dell should be one of the primary beneficiaries of upcoming enterprise datacenter refresh that looks particularly strong for 2026, AI infrastructure buildouts, and Win-10 end-of-life in which ~50% of units still need to be refreshed or remain vulnerable,” Piper Sandler analyst James Fish said.

However, Fish added that the company will face the secular headwind in the shift towards cloud by enterprises, refresh is a double-edged sword that could lead to market share losses persisting, and PC share has been falling not only in the shift towards Apple, but also to other PC makers.

Enphase Energy downgraded by Mizuho

Mizuho downgraded Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) to Neutral from Outperform with a $37 price target, reduced from $50, citing the solar company's declining market share in the U.S. amid tax credit changes, with limited offsets from new financial products or technology improvements.

"Our downgrade reflects reduced resi solar demand in 2026, lower solar market share due to lease switching, limited visibility into the success of a new lease+loan financing model for tail end of installers, and as cost declines from IQ9 tech adoption is limited to commercial market," Mizuho's Maheep Mandloi wrote.

Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) was downgraded by Barclays after the stock missed Q3 earnings. Analyst Andrew Mok downgraded the managed care firm to Underweight from Neutral and lowered its price target to $144 from $185 per share, citing risks to the company’s forward earnings per share.

Principal Financial Group (NASDAQ:PFG) stock was raised to Equal-weight from Underweight by Morgan Stanley, with the price target increased to $81.00 from $80.00, based on the current valuation. "The stock's multiple has compressed. Moreover, mitigated downside risks help ease the concerns about core business outlook," said analyst Bob Jian Huang in a research note.

Roth Capital Partners upgraded Core Scientific (NASDAQ:CORZ) shares to Buy from Neutral on the view that the odds of a merger with AI infrastructure provider CoreWeave (CRWV) are getting slimmer.

data.title: LendingClub, ABTC surge, Bitfarms, FHI slide: financials wrap
data.description:
Much of the price action across this week's biggest financial-stock movers reflected earnings season heating up.

LendingClub (LC) rose the most of any financial stock (with market cap over $2B) for the week ended Oct. 24, 2025, jumping 20%, after the online lending platform posted strong Q3 earnings, prompting an analyst upgrade;

American Bitcoin (ABTC), a bitcoin infrastructure platform, gapped up 19%;

Digital financial services provider Enova International (ENVA) gained 15% on the back of mixed Q3 results;

AI-powered lending platform Upstart Holdings (UPST) climbed 13%; and 

Stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL) rounded out the five biggest financial winners, advancing 12%.

On the negative side, Bitfarms (BITF), an operator of bitcoin (BTC-USD) data centers, retreated 8%;

Federated Hermes (FHI), which during the week said it agreed to buy a majority stake in private real estate investment manager FCP Fund Manager for up to $331M, slid 6.5%;

ServisFirst Bancshares (SFBS) dipped 5.7% after the lender turned in a double-miss on Q3 results;

Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL), despite delivering better-than-expected Q3 profit and sales, fell 5.6% this week; and

India's ICICI Bank (IBN) shed 5.1%.

data.title: 11 out of 14 consumer cyclical stocks deliver EPS wins this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
The consumer discretionary/cyclical sector was in focus this week as investors accessed earnings reports from companies including Tesla (TSLA) and General Motors (GM).

The consumer discretionary/cyclical sector rose nearly 7% so far this year, compared to the 15% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

In this week's earnings recap, 14 tickers reported results, out of which 11 reported earnings beat, while three missed estimates. On the revenue side, 12 companies exceeded revenue expectations, while two companies trailed estimates.

Below are the latest quarterly reports from some of the key players, which reported results this week:

Tesla (TSLA) released its Q3 earnings report on Wednesday. The electric vehicle giant reported revenue was up 11.6% year-over-year to $28.1 billion, while EPS came in at $0.50 vs. $0.56 consensus and $0.62 a year ago. However, the company warned of “near-term uncertainty from shifting trade, tariff, and fiscal policy.”

General Motors (GM) closed nearly 15% higher on Tuesday after having its best day in nearly 6 years, boosted by a top- and bottom-line beat in the third quarter. Even though, its profit took a hit, a combination of upbeat profit guidance and the automaker’s tariff outlook for the coming year impressed investors.

Similarly, Ford (F) also reported a third-quarter beat, with revenue reaching a record high of $50.5B, up 9% from a year ago. The automaker lowered FY25 profit guidance.

So far this quarter, 15 out of 130 companies that reported results are part of the consumer discretionary/cyclical sector.

Almost 80% of the consumer discretionary/cyclical sector companies that have reported results so far this quarter, recorded an EPS beat, while 87% beat on the top line.

For the upcoming week, notable consumer discretionary/cyclical sector that are scheduled to report quarterly results includes Starbucks (SBUX), eBay (EBAY) and Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL).

ETFs to keep a tab on consumer cyclicals: (NYSEARCA:XLY), (NYSEARCA:VCR), (NYSEARCA:FXD), (NYSEARCA:FDIS), (NYSEARCA:RSPD), and (NYSEARCA:RXI).

data.title: Amazon, Google, Microsoft likely to benefit from 'stable, healthy' cloud spending: UBS
data.description:
With earnings right around the corner, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) are likely to benefit from “stable” and “healthy” cloud spending, UBS said.

In speaking with 11 customers and partners at Oracle's (ORCL) AI World event, UBS analyst Karl Keirstead said the tone of the conversations about core cloud infrastructure spending was positive. Spending on artificial intelligence inference and training spending should provide the opportunity for upside as well, he added.

“We see minimal upside to Street estimates for AWS, with more upside potential in Azure and Google Cloud,” Keirstead wrote in a note to clients.

Delving deeper, he said the tone in the conversations was better than it was just three months ago, and the “progression in tone about overall cloud infra spending is notable.”

“While several of the [Fortune 500] enterprises spoke to flattish IT budgets in 2025, none – zero – were planning on incremental spending cuts or delays and in fact some partners said that they began seeing a loosening of budgets in 3Q, an 'unfreezing'”, Keirstead explained.

“While this diligence focused on the 85-90% of AWS, Azure and Google Cloud revs coming from enterprise customers ('core' demand), demand for cloud-hosted GPUs from AI model providers and start-ups remains outstanding, with no obvious slowdown in 3Q25.”

Other notable takeaways from the conversations include the fact that AI-induced pull-through of data software demand should help the hyperscalers and companies such as Snowflake (SNOW).

There is also the belief that Microsoft Azure is taking market share, as three partners said their Azure practices accelerated in the third-quarter or were expected to in the fourth-quarter. Conversely, the partners described their Amazon Web Services practices as “landing slightly below expectations” for the third-quarter, with forecasts of steady in the fourth quarter, Keirstead relayed.

Microsoft and Alphabet are set to report their quarterly results after the close of trading on Oct. 29, while Amazon will report its quarterly figures after the close of trading on Oct. 30.
